text: robotic assisted repair of recurrent right inguinal hernia with mesh by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a fifty-two year old male with history of right inguinal hernia repair ten years ago who presented with a painful right groin bulge and found to have recurrent right inguinal hernia. This video demonstrates the robotic assisted repair of recurrent right inguinal hernia with mesh and its challenges due to significant scarring secondary to the previous hernia repair. This video is also a testimony for valuable role of the robotic surgery in performing recurrent hernia repair.

The surgeon creates a transverse incision in the peritoneum, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The preperitoneal space is entered, and a preperitoneal flap is created, revealing significant scarring from the previous hernia repair as the surgery progresses medially towards the hernia defect.

The surgeon locates the recurrent hernia defect at the medial aspect of the previous repair site. Sutures from the prior hernia repair are visible. Using risted robotic instruments, the surgeon performs precise dissection amidst significant scarring without complications.
The surgeon places a three-dimensional mesh into the pre-peritoneal space, ensuring it adequately covers the hernia defect to prevent future recurrence.

The surgeon secures the mesh medially to Cooper's ligaments using 2-0 Ethibond sutures in a simple interrupted fashion. The remaining mesh is affixed to the abdominal wall with surgical glue. The peritoneal defect is closed primarily with an absorbable suture in a simple running fashion.
The surgeon includes the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat in the closure to reinforce the peritoneal flap, ensuring secure coverage of the repaired area.

This procedure was performed in a surgery center as an outpatient setting. He was discharged home the same day. The patient was followed two weeks after the surgery. He reported taking two days of pain medication after the operation. He had minimal discomfort with no swelling, seroma, hematoma in the groin region. Six weeks after the surgery he was released to perform heavy exercise and activity and he reported absolutely no discomfort in the right groin area.
This video is a testimony for valuable role of robotic platform in performing recurrent hernia repair.
